text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
ROUTINE SURVEILLANCE AND MAINTENANCE THERAPY FOLLOW-UP NOTE

**PATIENT:** Margaret Osei-Bonsu
**DOB/AGE:** 48 years old
**DIAGNOSIS:** High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0)
**VISIT TYPE:** Scheduled oncology follow-up — 24 months from initial diagnosis, 16 months on niraparib maintenance therapy

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 48-year-old woman with germline BRCA1-mutated, FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian/fallopian tube carcinoma who presents today for her scheduled surveillance and maintenance therapy visit. She is currently 24 months from her initial diagnosis and approximately 16 months into niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy at the dose-reduced level of 100 mg orally once daily. She reports doing well overall and has no new concerns today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was initially diagnosed with FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma at age 47, presenting with a four-month history of progressive abdominal distension, pelvic pressure, early satiety, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. Workup demonstrated bilateral ovarian masses (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm), omental caking, peritoneal carcinomatosis with implants up to 2.3 cm, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy, and a markedly elevated CA-125 of 3,847 U/mL. Germline testing confirmed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756ProfsTer74, the 5382insC founder mutation), and somatic tumor sequencing (Foundation One CDx) demonstrated loss of heterozygosity at the BRCA1 locus with a high HRD score of 68 and high genomic LOH of 31%, confirming homologous recombination deficiency.

She underwent primary debulking surgery with total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, resection of peritoneal implants, and right hemidiaphragmatic stripping, achieving complete gross resection (R0). Pathologic staging confirmed pT3c pN1 disease with 5 of 27 lymph nodes involved and no residual macroscopic disease at the operative margins. STIC lesion of the right fallopian tube fimbriated end was noted, supporting a fallopian tube origin of the primary.

She then completed six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) and paclitaxel (175 mg/m2, dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for cycles 5 and 6 due to cumulative Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy). Treatment was complicated by a Grade 2 carboplatin hypersensitivity reaction during cycle 2, successfully managed with a 12-step desensitization protocol. She also developed Grade 2 anemia requiring transfusion after cycle 4. She achieved complete biochemical (CA-125 normalized to 11 U/mL) and complete radiographic response (no evidence of disease on post-chemotherapy restaging CT) following six cycles.

Niraparib maintenance therapy was initiated approximately 8 months from initial diagnosis, at a starting dose of 200 mg daily per weight-based dosing (weight < 77 kg). Within the first month, she developed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 58,000/uL), requiring niraparib to be held. Platelets recovered and niraparib was restarted at a dose-reduced level of 100 mg daily approximately 5 weeks later. Subsequent monitoring has shown persistent mild-to-moderate thrombocytopenia (range approximately 95,000–142,000/uL, Grade 1–2), which has been stable and clinically manageable at the 100 mg dose. She also developed niraparib-associated hypertension, requiring initiation of amlodipine 5 mg daily, subsequently uptitrated to 10 mg daily, with good blood pressure control. She had a 12-month on-maintenance CT scan approximately 4 months ago, which showed no evidence of recurrence.

Today, at her 24-month visit, Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports no new symptoms. She specifically denies any abdominal pain, bloating, early satiety, change in bowel habits, urinary symptoms, pelvic pressure, or weight loss. She reports stable mild tingling in the tips of her bilateral toes, unchanged from prior visits, which she describes as minimally bothersome and not interfering with her activities. She denies any bleeding, bruising, petechiae, or epistaxis. She denies headache, chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. She has been tolerating the niraparib 100 mg at bedtime without significant nausea. She mentions that she has been very active — recently resumed hiking on local trails and is attending weekly yoga classes. She returned to full-time teaching several months ago and is managing her professional and personal responsibilities without difficulty.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No significant weight change. Energy levels acceptable.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea or vomiting at baseline. No abdominal pain or distension. No change in bowel habits. No blood in stool.
Genitourinary: No urinary frequency, urgency, or hematuria. No pelvic pressure or pain. (Status post TAH-BSO — no menses.)
Musculoskeletal: Persistent bilateral distal lower extremity tingling (toes), stable Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy, non-limiting.
Neurological: No new neurological complaints. No headache beyond previously noted mild intermittent episodes now resolved. No cognitive changes.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema. Blood pressure has been stable per home monitoring.
Hematologic: No bleeding, bruising, or petechiae. No epistaxis. No gum bleeding.
Skin: No new rashes, alopecia resolved with hair fully regrown.
Psychosocial: Patient reports improved emotional wellbeing. Continues active participation in gynecologic oncology support group. Anxiety well-managed; no formal psychiatric concerns at this time.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

PAST MEDICAL AND ONCOLOGIC HISTORY

1. High-grade serous carcinoma, bilateral ovaries/fallopian tubes, FIGO IIIC, germline BRCA1 (c.5266dupC) — as above
2. Carboplatin hypersensitivity (Grade 2, cycle 2 — managed with desensitization)
3. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (sensory, bilateral — Grade 2 at nadir, now Grade 1 residual)
4. Niraparib-associated Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (dose reduced to 100 mg daily)
5. Niraparib-associated hypertension (on amlodipine 10 mg daily)
6. Iron deficiency anemia post-chemotherapy (resolved)
7. Right renal calculus (4 mm, passed spontaneously approximately 6 months ago)

SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Primary debulking surgery (TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic/para-aortic LND, peritoneal implant resection, right hemidiaphragmatic stripping) — R0 resection

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Maternal aunt: breast cancer, diagnosed age 38
- Maternal grandmother: ovarian cancer, diagnosed age 55
- Patient's daughter (age 22): confirmed BRCA1 carrier, referred for risk-reduction counseling
- Patient's sister (age 44): referred for genetic counseling

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Secondary school mathematics teacher; full-time
- Born in Accra, Ghana; emigrated to the United States at age 22
- Never smoker; no alcohol use
- Two children; lives with family
- Active: hiking, yoga

ALLERGIES:
- Carboplatin — Grade 2 hypersensitivity reaction (flushing, urticaria, chest tightness); managed with desensitization protocol (no longer clinically relevant as chemotherapy has concluded)

MEDICATIONS:
1. Niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily at bedtime
2. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily (for niraparib-associated hypertension)
3. Duloxetine 30 mg orally once daily (for residual chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy)
4. Ondansetron 8 mg orally as needed (PRN nausea with niraparib — rarely used)
5. Multivitamin daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 128/80 mmHg (well-controlled)
- Heart rate: 74 bpm, regular
- Temperature: 98.4°F (36.9°C)
- Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
- Weight: 65.2 kg (stable; baseline 64 kg at diagnosis)
- ECOG Performance Status: 1 (though patient reports functional status consistent with ECOG 0 in daily life)
- SpO2: 98% on room air

General: Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Hair fully regrown. Appears in good health.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No pallor of conjunctivae. Mucous membranes moist. No oral lesions. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No pleural rub.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed midline laparotomy scar. No palpable hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No palpable masses. No shifting dullness or fluid wave to suggest ascites. Bowel sounds present and normal in all quadrants.

Pelvic (limited external exam per patient preference, deferred formal bimanual given post-hysterectomy status and no symptoms): No inguinal lymphadenopathy. External genitalia unremarkable. No pelvic masses appreciated on external assessment.

Lymph nodes: No supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy palpated.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. Mild decreased sensation to light touch at distal bilateral toes — consistent with Grade 1 residual peripheral sensory neuropathy. No evidence of ecchymoses, petechiae, or purpura. No skin breakdown.

Neurological: Alert, oriented, conversational. No focal deficits. Gait steady.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CA-125: 7 U/mL (normal range < 35 U/mL) — persistently normal and stable
HE4: Not repeated this visit (was normal at last check)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.3 x10^3/uL (normal)
- ANC: 3.2 x10^3/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 12.1 g/dL (low-normal; stable)
- Hematocrit: 36.5%
- MCV: 88 fL
- Platelets: 114,000/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia per CTCAE; chronic, stable at this level on niraparib 100 mg; no clinical bleeding)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 25 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 0.81 mg/dL (eGFR > 60 mL/min/1.73m2)
- ALT: 22 U/L
- AST: 19 U/L
- Alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
- Total protein: 7.2 g/dL
- Albumin: 4.1 g/dL
- Glucose: 92 mg/dL

Thyroid function (checked at last visit — normal; not repeated today).
Coagulation panel: Not obtained (no clinical indication).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent imaging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast, performed approximately 4 months ago at the 12-month on-maintenance milestone. That scan demonstrated no evidence of disease recurrence — no peritoneal implants, no lymphadenopathy, no ascites, no new masses. Post-surgical changes in the pelvis were stable. Findings were entirely consistent with sustained complete remission on niraparib maintenance. The next surveillance CT is planned for approximately 4 months from today (at the 24-month post-chemotherapy completion timepoint).

No new imaging obtained today.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 48-year-old woman with germline BRCA1-mutated (5382insC), FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian/fallopian tube carcinoma. She achieved complete gross resection at primary debulking surgery and a complete biochemical and radiographic response to six cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel. She has now been on niraparib maintenance therapy for 16 months at a dose-reduced level of 100 mg daily, and she is currently at 24 months from her initial diagnosis. She remains in sustained complete remission with a CA-125 of 7 U/mL and no clinical, laboratory, or recent imaging evidence of disease.

1. ONCOLOGIC STATUS — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE
CA-125 stable and normal at 7 U/mL; has been persistently normal for over 14 months. No clinical signs or symptoms of recurrence on today's detailed history and physical examination. She is doing exceptionally well. I find this reassuring given her original high-risk staging — FIGO IIIC with extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental cake, and positive lymph nodes — and note that the combination of R0 resection and the favorable biology conferred by her germline BRCA1 status with high HRD tumor profile has translated into durable remission thus far. We will continue to monitor carefully but her trajectory is very encouraging.

2. NIRAPARIB MAINTENANCE THERAPY — CONTINUING AT 100 MG DAILY
She continues niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily at bedtime. This dose was established after an early Grade 3 thrombocytopenia event requiring temporary hold and dose reduction from the starting 200 mg dose. At 100 mg, she has demonstrated stable Grade 1 (occasionally Grade 1-2) thrombocytopenia (platelets today 114,000/uL), which is chronic and manageable — no bleeding, no clinical consequences. We will continue niraparib at 100 mg without further dose modification today. Monthly CBC monitoring will continue. Total planned duration of maintenance is 3 years from initiation, consistent with the SOLO-1 trial data demonstrating a significant progression-free survival benefit (median PFS not reached in the treatment arm at median 41-month follow-up) in patients with germline BRCA1/2 mutations achieving complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. We are currently at 16 of a planned 36 months.

3. NIRAPARIB-ASSOCIATED HYPERTENSION
Blood pressure today is 128/80 mmHg, well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg daily. No dose changes to antihypertensives needed. Will continue monitoring at each visit.

4. CHEMOTHERAPY-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY
Residual Grade 1 bilateral distal lower extremity sensory neuropathy remains stable — subjectively improved from the Grade 2 severity seen during active chemotherapy. She has mild tingling in the toes only with no functional impairment. She is able to hike and practice yoga without limitation. Duloxetine 30 mg daily continued. No change in management at this time. I do not anticipate further significant improvement at this stage, but the stable Grade 1 course is acceptable given prior paclitaxel dose reduction and time since completion of chemotherapy.

5. SURVEILLANCE IMAGING
Next surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast is scheduled for approximately 4 months from today, corresponding to the 24-month post-chemotherapy completion timepoint. This is consistent with our surveillance protocol for HGSC in remission on maintenance therapy.

6. BRCA1-RELATED BREAST CANCER SURVEILLANCE
Given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, the patient should continue annual breast MRI and annual digital mammography beginning now (NCCN guidelines recommend annual breast MRI starting at age 25, alternating with mammography every 6 months, or simultaneously in high-risk patients). Previous breast MRI performed at an outside institution was reported as normal (report on file). Discussed risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy: patient previously declined and continues to decline at this time, preferring intensive surveillance. I have reiterated my support for her decision and confirmed the surveillance plan. Next breast MRI should be scheduled within the next 6 months.

7. FAMILY GENETIC TESTING UPDATE
Patient's daughter (age 22) was confirmed as a BRCA1 carrier and has been referred for risk-reduction counseling — I commend the patient for facilitating this important cascade testing. Patient's sister (age 44) was also referred for genetic counseling and testing. No update on sister's result today. Patient is engaged and knowledgeable about the implications of the BRCA1 mutation for her family members.

8. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND QUALITY OF LIFE
Patient reports good emotional wellbeing and is actively engaged in the oncology support group. She has returned fully to professional life as a mathematics teacher and continues her active hobbies. Her overall quality of life appears excellent. No formal psychiatric or social work referral indicated at this time. I continue to acknowledge the emotional toll of living with an advanced cancer diagnosis and the ongoing uncertainty of surveillance, and I offered a referral for supportive counseling which she respectfully declined.

9. RETURN VISIT
The patient will return in approximately 3 months for routine surveillance blood work (CA-125, CBC, metabolic panel), with CT imaging in approximately 4 months as noted above. She has my contact information and that of the on-call team, and has been instructed to call immediately if she develops new abdominal pain, bloating, distension, early satiety, pelvic pressure, unexplained weight loss, or other concerning symptoms.

Total time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes, including counseling and care coordination.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Gynecologic Oncology / Medical Oncology
